title: Mind Games at the White House: Mentalist Oz Pearlman to Perform at Correspondents' Dinner
summary:
Imagine sitting in a room full of politicians and journalists, and a guy walks on stage claiming he can read minds. Sounds like a magic show, right? Well, that's exactly what's happening at the White House Correspondents' Association Dinner this Saturday—and it's a huge break from tradition.

For over a century, this dinner has featured comedians like George Carlin, Jay Leno, and Stephen Colbert, who would roast (make fun of) the president and other politicians. But this year, the headliner is Oz Pearlman, a 43-year-old mentalist. A mentalist is a type of magician who creates the illusion of mind-reading—guessing people's PIN numbers, secrets, or even what they're thinking. Pearlman went viral for his short, rapid-fire tricks on late-night shows and social media, and he's performed for Fortune 500 CEOs and professional athletes.

Pearlman told NPR that when he first got the call, he thought it was a prank. But he soon understood the intent: "My hope for the dinner, and why they brought me there instead of a comedian to roast people, is that my job is to bring us together." He says people in the room may disagree on politics, war, and the economy, but for 25 minutes, they'll laugh, applaud, and have their jaws drop.

What makes this year extra buzzy is that President Trump plans to attend—his first appearance at the dinner as president. Pearlman hints that Trump won't just be watching; he'll be participating in the act. "Reading Donald Trump's mind is arguably the most impressive thing you could ever do," Pearlman says. He's been planning this moment for 10 years, thinking through "every minutia" of how it will play out.

So, how do mentalists "read minds"? They don't actually read thoughts. According to experts, they use research, suggestion, showmanship, and sometimes even hidden information (like a phone or a Google search). As one professor put it, "I can say with some confidence it's not coming through reading your facial expressions."

Will Pearlman pull off a jaw-dropping moment? If he does, he says, "Saturday night ... will be the reason you talk about me for years to come." And everyone will be asking the same question: How did he do that?
why_it_matters: This story highlights how media events adapt to the times, and how entertainment can sometimes bridge political divides.

keywords.explanation: A performer who creates the illusion of mind-reading through trickery and showmanship.
The official residence and workplace of the U.S. president in Washington, D.C.
The White House Correspondents' Association Dinner, an annual media and politics event.
Oz Pearlman, a 43-year-old mentalist known for viral tricks on late-night shows.
President Donald Trump, who will attend and possibly participate in the act.
The aspect of a person that thinks, used in the context of mind-reading illusions.

background_read: The White House Correspondents' Dinner has been held since 1920 and is a key event for media and politics.
Past comedians like Stephen Colbert and Jimmy Kimmel have used the platform to roast presidents.
Mentalism as entertainment has roots in stage magic and psychological tricks.
Oz Pearlman is known for viral videos where he guesses PIN numbers and secrets, performing for celebrities and business leaders.

TRADITION: Over a century of comedians roasting presidents
└─ examples: George Carlin, Jay Leno, Stephen Colbert

├─ Pearlman's hope: to bring people together
└─ His viral fame: short tricks on late-night shows

HOW IT WORKS: Mentalists use research, suggestion, showmanship
└─ Expert quote: 'not coming through reading facial expressions'
└─ Quote: 'Saturday night ... will be the reason you talk about me'

perspectives.description: Pearlman's performance could be a fun, unifying moment in a politically divided environment.
Some critics may find mentalism gimmicky or less substantive than the usual political satire.
This choice reflects a trend towards non-traditional entertainment at formal events, blending magic with politics.
If the performance goes viral, it could increase interest in mentalism and change the future format of the dinner.
